source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 7.7-7.8 config files & su (pp. 119-124)

questions.q: How is the /etc/default/useradd file meant to be edited?
options: With the useradd -D command (running useradd -D alone displays its contents)
By hand with a text editor only
questions.why: useradd -D both displays and sets the defaults in /etc/default/useradd; the other config files (login.defs, skel) are edited by hand.

questions.q: In /etc/login.defs, UMASK 022 means a newly created FILE gets which permissions?
options: rw-r--r-- (644) - the base 666 minus the mask, with no execute bit on new files
questions.why: UMASK 022 would give 755 for directories, but new files never get the execute bit, so they come out 644 (rw-r--r--).

questions.q: According to /etc/login.defs, which UID range is reserved for system (service) accounts?
questions.why: SYS_UID_MIN..SYS_UID_MAX is 201-999; regular users get UID_MIN..UID_MAX (1000-60000).

questions.q: What is the /etc/skel directory used for?
options: It holds template files (.bashrc, .bash_profile, .bash_logout) copied into each new user's home directory
It contains the system's skeleton kernel
It is where passwords are cached
questions.why: Everything in /etc/skel is copied into a new user's home directory at creation, seeding their environment files.

questions.q: Why does 'userdel -r' also delete the user's primary group?
options: Because USERGROUPS_ENAB is set to yes in /etc/login.defs (private-group scheme)
It does not - the group is always kept
questions.why: USERGROUPS_ENAB yes ties the same-named private primary group to the user, so removing the user removes that group too.

questions.q: Switch identity to the user alain, loading their complete environment.
questions.why: su - <login> switches user AND loads their full login environment (the dash is what does that).

questions.q: What is the key difference between 'su root' and 'su - root'?
options: 'su -' loads the target user's full login environment (PWD, HOME, PATH...); plain 'su' keeps your current environment
'su -' does not ask for a password; 'su' does
questions.why: Without the dash, su keeps your existing environment (e.g. PWD and LOGNAME stay); with the dash it loads the target's complete environment - so don't forget the -.

questions.q: What does the -c option of su do, as in 'su - root -c "passwd alain"'?
options: Runs the given command under the other user's identity, then returns
questions.why: su -c executes a single command as the target user without starting an interactive shell.
